topic: Someone finds out they have a half-sibling they never knew about
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official letter, thick and cream-colored, lay crumpled on the antique mahogany desk. Elias picked it up with two fingers, as if handling something contaminated. His jaw tightened, the muscle jumping beneath his skin. It was a genealogical report, and it confirmed the impossible: a sibling. A half-sibling. The sheer absurdity of it made him want to laugh, but a cold, hard knot formed in his stomach instead. He smoothed the paper flat, his gaze sweeping over the unfamiliar names and dates. There was no warmth in this revelation, only a sharp sense of intrusion. He stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room, his movements tight and jerky. "Unnecessary," he muttered to the empty air, the sound low and brittle. He tossed the document onto the floor, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet study. He needed to burn it, to erase this unwelcome complication from his meticulously ordered life.

[story 2]

I stared at the grainy photograph. It was a stranger, undeniably familiar in the set of their jaw, yet utterly alien. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the breath from my lungs. A connection I never knew existed, a branch of my family tree I’d been completely blind to. My hands clenched into fists at my sides, the knuckles turning white against my skin. I didn't feel surprise; I felt a profound sense of inconvenience. This was just… extra baggage. I turned away from the picture, focusing instead on the precise arrangement of the books on the shelf, cataloging them with an almost aggressive focus. When my mother called, my voice was clipped, devoid of inflection. "I'll call you back later," I managed, already formulating a strategy to minimize the fallout. This entire situation felt like a poorly constructed puzzle I was now forced to solve, and I was thoroughly unimpressed by the pieces.

[story 3]

He slid the file across the table with a deliberate lack of regard. His expression was one of detached observation, as if examining a particularly dull insect. I watched him, my shoulders subtly rising toward my ears. Every line of his posture communicated a clear assessment: this discovery was beneath his notice, an irritating distraction from whatever important thing he was actually doing. I picked up the report, my fingers brushing against the paper as if it were soiled. The information detailed a life I hadn't known existed, a parallel existence I was now obligated to acknowledge. A slow, almost imperceptible sneer touched the corner of my mouth. "How utterly tedious," I remarked, my tone flat, letting the weight of my boredom settle between us. I didn't need to dwell on it; it was simply an unwelcome footnote in someone else's history.
